item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article has a solid core. There is something quite enchanting about the simplicity of 'a guy chasing you'. I like how the German is characterised by how he changes over time - there is a subtle dread to knowing that he is improving his craft. I really appreciate that the excerpts of the book are not told from his perspective - just a little drip of characterisation every now and then was enough.

Beyond the core, though, things are a little lacking. I wish it had been a little clearer in-universe on why those book excerpts were worth attaching. I found the excerpts from the journal unsatisfying. The points they introduced felt too convenient for cheap character development - not unforgivable, seeing as they are excerpts after all - but to me they didn't feel like entries of a journal. The decline was far too linear for excerpts taken more than 100 days apart. It's fine that this was used to explain why the character failed in their task, but it would have been a good opportunity for passive characterisation of the German.

I am tired of "oh no! it got worse!" as a cliffhanger. I don't feel like I've gained anything by knowing that there is undeveloped plot, and I don't think it's worth the confusion at the start of trying to decide whether or not I care about reading the previous conprocs/desc or if I just want to get into the flow of the article. There's also a divergence that I'd've liked to have seen developed. A lot of stuff happens during the dream described as if it happens in the real world. I'd like to know what actually happened.

Novote overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a swordsmanship nerd, I really enjoyed this. And wow! You’re actually a master of the technique? That’s super cool! +1 all the way!

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This isn't a mistranslation. Tutta porta di ferro is the 7th guard of Fiore dei Liberi's fencing manual.

It may not be "correct" in modern Italian, but it's an actual stance.

I did not try and backtranslate anything. I'm a real-life swordsmaster of this fencing system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this one, the story here is fun to follow, I can definitely see your love of swordplay shining through this all over. The second addendum read a little repetitively, only because almost every paragraph started with some variation of "I am/did xyz". But that's a minor nitpick.

As a whole, the article itself is okay, if nothing spectacular. It has a concept executed well, no one section really stood out to me as something I particularly enjoyed or disliked. The formatting choices here make the presentation pop, and kept me wanting to read more. Not only because it was pretty but the use of collapsibles and tabs to keep the text in short bursts increased the readability by a lot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So if I'm getting this right, the last battle the swordsman used the same form as the original guy that defeated the German, so he thought it was him. His rage from being defeated again sent him into the real world

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hello, Italian user here, I really never write on the site but I read (and lurk) a lot.
This time as I thin it could be useful, I need to make some statement about the use of my native language here. Just excuse me if I'm wordy, I'm just not very used to write in English.

[[html]]
Cpt. DeLuca reaches the bottom of the stairs, and stands across from SCP-4621-2, in a defensive stance, detailed as "Tutta porta di ferro" (full iron door).
[[/html]]

"Tutta porta di ferro" is almost nosense in Italian. As I understand you tried to backward translate "full iron door" back in italian, but we word that kind of thing a bit different. I would suggest "Portone di ferro" as a more friendly and meaning-wise translation of "full iron door". Also a more literal one could be "Portone di ferro massiccio" (massiccio for the "full" part).

Anyways if we want to conceive an appropriate name for a defensive stance in Italian, "Muro di Ferro" or "Iron Wall" in english would bettere fit it. The wall is better suited to convey the meaning of something that should be defensive in Italian, although a big door ("Portone") could also easily fit the role.

Translation aside, really a nice work of narrative here and a very nice story-behind-the-scp. A well deserved +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn that is some GUD formatting. Holy guacamole.

When I saw this in #critters, my only serious critique was the ‘informal’ dictation of the final log and I bought this up. Lines like

Cpt. DeLuca cautiously advances down the stairs, keeping his sword in front of him the entire time. SCP-4621-2 appears transfixed, his eyes locked on to Cpt. DeLuca's sword.

Are a far cry from the detached, stenographer prose that alot of us expect, and I have voted because of this before in other works. However, I don't mind it here. The article is very sword-focused, and it would be a shame to miss out on a sword fight. Furthermore…clinical tone isn’t exactly broken. And that’s thanks to something that has changed since #critters, the addition of:

BEGIN AETHERIC TRANSCRIPTION…

And the accompanying description of this term.

Hecking stuff. +1 for entertaining stories, entertaining formatting, and entertaining solutions to creating entertaining stories.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Man, it's been a while.

So credit for this goes out to my wife, primarily and Soulless for helping me storyboard this one.

This is based on a recurring nightmare I've had since I was 16. The guy chasing me really is getting better too.

I hope you enjoy.

EDIT: Also, this is my entry in to my own contest, which I can't win. But I figured I'd put something up for it :)
